title_en: Fabricated, Rejected, and Irregular Hadith Types
content_en:
Among the most important categories for the practicing Muslim to understand are those that describe the lowest grades of hadith — reports that should not be cited as evidence and, in the most severe cases, must be actively warned against. Al-Bayquniyyah discusses several such categories, of which the Mawdu' (fabricated), the Munkar (rejected), and the Shadhdh (irregular/anomalous) are among the most significant.

The Mawdu' (fabricated) hadith is the most severe category of da'if. It is a report that was not actually said, done, or approved by the Prophet (peace be upon him) but was falsely attributed to him by a liar. The hadith scholars identified fabrications through two main methods: the internal evidence of the text (matn), and the external evidence of the chain (isnad). Textual evidence of fabrication includes: content that contradicts firmly established facts or principles of Islamic faith; exaggerated promises of reward or threats of punishment for minor acts; clumsily rhymed or metrically composed language that does not resemble prophetic speech; or historical anachronisms that could not have been known in the Prophet's time. Chain evidence includes: the presence of known fabricators in the chain, admission of fabrication by a transmitter, or impossibility of the claimed teacher-student relationship given the transmitters' dates.

The hadith scholars compiled extensive works listing fabricated hadiths, most notably Al-Mawdu'at of Imam Ibn al-Jawzi (d. 597 AH) and Al-La'ali al-Masnu'ah of Imam al-Suyuti (d. 911 AH). These works identified thousands of fabrications that had entered circulation and warned the Muslim community against attributing them to the Prophet. The motivation for hadith fabrication varied: some fabricators were pious but misguided individuals who invented hadiths encouraging good deeds; others were partisans who fabricated reports supporting their political or theological positions; others were simple liars seeking fame or material reward. The hadith scholars were unforgiving toward all these categories, since the Prophet (peace be upon him) warned: 'Whoever attributes to me something I did not say, let him take his seat in the Fire.'

The Munkar (rejected/denounced) hadith is one narrated by a weak transmitter that contradicts what reliable transmitters have reported. The term 'munkar' carries the sense of something that is 'denied' or 'disowned' — a report that clashes with the established body of reliable hadith. When a single weak narrator transmits a report whose content conflicts with what stronger narrators have transmitted on the same or related topics, the isolated weak narration is classified as munkar. The Munkar is distinct from the Shadhdh in that Shadhdh involves a reliable narrator contradicting one who is more reliable or more numerous, while Munkar involves a weak narrator contradicting reliable ones.

The Shadhdh (anomalous/irregular) hadith is one in which a reliable (thiqa) transmitter contradicts other transmitters who are more numerous or more reliable. The term comes from the Arabic for something that is isolated or deviates from the group. A hadith may have an excellent isnad — all transmitters are reliable — yet still be shadhdh if that chain stands alone against other chains that transmit a different version of the same report. In such cases, the majority or more authoritative version is accepted and the isolated version, even from a reliable narrator, is classified as shadhdh. This reflects the hadith scholars' understanding that reliability of individual transmitters is not sufficient; the convergence of multiple independent chains provides additional confirmation that increases confidence in a report.

These negative categories — Mawdu', Munkar, and Shadhdh — demonstrate the intellectual sophistication of the hadith sciences. The scholars were not content with a simple binary of 'authentic' and 'unauthentic' but developed a nuanced taxonomy that distinguished the reasons for weakness, the degree of unreliability, and the specific nature of the defect. This taxonomy served practical purposes: it told scholars how to respond to particular reports, whether by rejecting them outright (Mawdu'), by treating them with extreme caution (Munkar), or by favoring one version over another (Shadhdh). The study of these categories through al-Bayquniyyah and its commentaries equips students to navigate the hadith literature with discernment and scholarly precision.
content_ar: الموضوع أدنى مراتب الحديث وهو المختلق المصنوع، والمنكر ما رواه الضعيف مخالفاً للثقة، والشاذ ما رواه الثقة مخالفاً لمن هو أوثق منه.
